text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- URGENT FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Disease Reassessment and Second-Line Therapy Planning

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Hematology/Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Urgent Return Visit (Disease Progression Counseling)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Okafor is a 58-year-old Nigerian-American male with known stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS) with bilobar hepatic metastases, now presenting urgently for discussion of new imaging findings demonstrating disease progression with new bilateral pulmonary metastases and hepatic progression while on maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus Avastin.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Okafor was initially diagnosed approximately 10.5 months ago with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. His tumor was characterized as microsatellite stable (MSS) and harbored a KRAS G12D activating mutation, as well as co-alterations including TP53 R248W, APC loss, SMAD4 R361H (adverse prognostic marker), PIK3CA E545K, and PTEN loss on comprehensive genomic profiling. These features rendered anti-EGFR therapy contraindicated and immunotherapy unlikely to be of benefit.

He received first-line induction chemotherapy with modified FOLFOX6 plus Avastin for 8 cycles (approximately 4 months), achieving a partial response with roughly 40% reduction in total tumor burden. His course was complicated by grade 3 cumulative Eloxatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy at cycle 8, necessitating oxali discontinuation. Given his pre-existing baseline diabetic peripheral neuropathy at treatment onset, this complication was not unexpected. He was de-escalated to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus Avastin (essentially a FOLFOX-without-Eloxatin strategy). He also underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy approximately 7 months ago for resection of the primary tumor, with uncomplicated postoperative recovery. He then resumed maintenance 5-FU/LV plus bev approximately 8 months ago.

At his most recent routine follow-up approximately 1.5 months ago (9 months post-diagnosis), he was clinically stable: ECOG PS 1, CEA at that time was 35.2 ng/mL, and he was tolerating maintenance therapy reasonably well. However, approximately 3 weeks ago, he began noticing new mild dyspnea on exertion -- he reports being winded climbing two flights of stairs, which had not been a complaint previously. He also noted subjectively increasing fatigue over the past 4-6 weeks. This prompted early restaging CT, which was performed approximately 2 weeks ago and demonstrated concerning new findings.

The restaging CT at approximately 10 months showed new bilateral pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 8 mm, left upper lobe 6 mm, right middle lobe 5 mm), all not present on prior imaging, as well as modest but definite increases in all four hepatic target lesions (sum of target lesions increased from 4.5 cm at nadir to 5.5 cm, a 22% increase). His CEA had risen from a nadir of 35.2 ng/mL to 48.7 ng/mL at that visit. Given concern for new pulmonary metastases versus an alternative etiology, PET/CT was performed approximately 10-14 days ago and showed FDG-avidity in all bilateral pulmonary nodules (SUVmax ranging 2.1 to 3.4) as well as ongoing FDG-avid hepatic disease (dominant segment VII lesion SUVmax 6.1). The anastomotic site was negative for recurrence. No additional sites of disease were identified.

Today's CEA returned at 62.3 ng/mL (up from 48.7 ng/mL two weeks ago, and from a nadir of 35.2 ng/mL). CA 19-9 is now 148 U/mL (up from 89 U/mL at the 6-month mark). The combination of rising tumor markers, FDG-avid new pulmonary lesions, and hepatic progression is consistent with disease progression on maintenance Avastin-containing therapy.

He denies hemoptysis, chest pain, pleuritic pain, or cough. His dyspnea on exertion is mild and stable. He has not noticed any new abdominal pain or change in bowel habits since the colectomy. He reports no fever, chills, or night sweats. He continues gabapentin 300 mg TID for neuropathic symptoms in his feet and, to a lesser extent, his hands, with modest benefit. He returned to part-time work in an administrative capacity at his high school. He is accompanied today by his wife.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Increased fatigue, grade 2 (worse over the past 4-6 weeks; reports needing to rest more during the day). No significant unintentional weight loss. No fever or chills.
Respiratory: Mild dyspnea on exertion (grade 1), new over approximately 3 weeks. No cough, hemoptysis, or chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: No abdominal pain. Bowel function regular since sigmoid colectomy (1-2 formed stools per day). No rectal bleeding or change in caliber. No nausea.
Neurological: Ongoing bilateral peripheral neuropathy (numbness and tingling in feet bilaterally, lesser degree in hands; balance impairment requiring caution on uneven surfaces). Stable compared to last visit.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain.
Cardiovascular: Occasional mild headaches, historically correlated with blood pressure fluctuations. No chest pain at rest. No lower extremity edema.
Skin: No wound breakdown at colectomy site. Mild persistent alopecia (grade 1).
Genitourinary: No hematuria. No dysuria.
All other systems reviewed and negative unless noted above.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
(Previously documented in detail; summarized here)
- Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases -- diagnosed approximately 10.5 months ago
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- well-controlled on metformin; HbA1c last checked approximately 6 months ago (within normal limits per patient report)
- Hypertension -- now on triple antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 5 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg not yet added at this visit -- see below); blood pressure elevated on current two-drug regimen
- Hyperlipidemia -- on statin (not documented as active treatment in oncology record but patient confirms he remains on it)
- Pre-existing bilateral peripheral neuropathy (diabetic, baseline grade 1 at cancer diagnosis; worsened to grade 3 during oxali therapy, now grade 2 on gabapentin)
- Former smoker (quit 12 years ago, 15 pack-year history)

SURGICAL HISTORY
- Port-a-cath placement (right chest wall, subclavian approach) -- approximately 10 months ago
- Laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis -- approximately 7 months ago; final pathology pT3 N2a

---

SOCIAL HISTORY
Mr. Okafor is married and lives with his wife. He is currently working part-time as a high school principal in an administrative capacity (reduced from full-time). He has two adult children. He is a former smoker with a 15 pack-year history, quit 12 years ago. He drinks alcohol socially (approximately 1-2 drinks on weekends; none during active chemotherapy per patient report). He is of Nigerian-American descent. He has a strong support network.

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of colorectal cancer. No family history of Lynch syndrome-associated malignancies. Father had hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Mother had breast cancer in her 70s (unknown receptor status).

ALLERGIES
NKDA (no known drug allergies). Patient has tolerated bev, 5FU, Eloxatin, and ondansetron without allergic reactions.

MEDICATIONS
1. Adrucil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours every 14 days (maintenance)
2. Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1 every 14 days (maintenance)
3. bev 5 mg/kg IV day 1 every 14 days (maintenance -- last administered approximately 10 days ago)
4. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID (diabetes)
5. Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily (hypertension)
6. Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (hypertension -- added during first-line therapy for bev-related hypertension)
7. Gabapentin 300 mg PO TID (peripheral neuropathy)
8. Atorvastatin (dose not on record; patient taking per primary care)
9. Loperamide 2 mg PO PRN (for diarrhea, prescribed during prior treatment; patient rarely uses currently)
10. Ondansetron 8 mg PO PRN (antiemetic for infusion days)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational and engaged in discussion. Accompanied by wife.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg (right arm, seated). Heart rate 78 bpm, regular. Respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute. Temperature 37.0 degrees C. SpO2 97% on room air. Weight 86.2 kg (down approximately 1.5 kg from prior visit; patient attributes to dietary changes). BMI approximately 28.0 kg/m2.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Anicteric sclerae. Oropharynx without mucositis or lesions. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.
Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rhonchi, or crackles. Breathing unlabored at rest. No dullness to percussion.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds present in all four quadrants. No palpable hepatomegaly (liver edge not percussible below right costal margin). No splenomegaly. Well-healed laparoscopic port-site scars and lower midline incision from sigmoid colectomy; no wound erythema, dehiscence, or hernia palpable.
Extremities: No lower extremity pitting edema. No calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.
Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation: decreased light touch and proprioception in bilateral feet (stocking distribution) and mild decrease in distal bilateral hands (fingertip level). Romberg mildly positive. Gait slightly wide-based and cautious but independent.
Skin: Mild diffuse hair thinning consistent with prior 5-FU/alopecia (grade 1). Port site clean and intact. No jaundice or palmar erythema.
Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete blood count: WBC 6.4 K/uL, hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL (mild anemia, slight decrease from 12.8 g/dL previously; likely anemia of chronic disease), platelets 234 K/uL.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, creatinine 0.92 mg/dL (eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2), BUN 14 mg/dL, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, ALT 32 U/L, AST 28 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 98 U/L. Albumin 3.8 g/dL. Liver function tests within normal limits.
Glucose: 124 mg/dL (fasting; slightly elevated, consistent with known DM2).
Coagulation: PT/INR not formally checked today; previously normal.
Tumor markers:
  - CEA: 62.3 ng/mL (up from nadir 35.2 ng/mL; previously 48.7 ng/mL two weeks ago -- progressive uptrend)
  - CA 19-9: 148 U/mL (up from 89 U/mL at 6-month check, and from 72.4 U/mL at 9-month visit)
Thyroid function: Not checked today.
HbA1c: Not rechecked today; patient states last value was acceptable per primary care.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (performed approximately 2 weeks ago): Previously resected sigmoid colon -- no anastomotic recurrence. All four hepatic target lesions have grown modestly compared to nadir measurements: segment II now 0.9 cm (was 0.7 cm), segment IVa now 1.3 cm (was 1.1 cm), segment VI now 1.1 cm (was 0.9 cm at nadir), and the dominant segment VII lesion now 2.2 cm (was 1.8 cm at nadir). In aggregate, the hepatic lesions are clearly larger than at nadir, though still substantially reduced compared to where they started (segment VII began at 3.2 cm at diagnosis). The more striking new finding is the appearance of three bilateral pulmonary nodules -- an 8 mm nodule in the right lower lobe, a 6 mm nodule in the left upper lobe, and a 5 mm nodule in the right middle lobe -- none of which were present on the prior scan. No ascites or peritoneal implants were seen.

PET/CT (performed approximately 10 days ago): All three pulmonary nodules light up on FDG-PET imaging -- right lower lobe SUVmax 3.4, left upper lobe SUVmax 2.9, right middle lobe SUVmax 2.1. The FDG-avidity in these small nodules in the setting of a known metastatic colorectal primary is highly consistent with viable metastatic disease. All hepatic lesions remain FDG-avid as well, with the segment VII lesion most active at SUVmax 6.1. There is no new FDG-avid disease in the bones, peritoneum, adrenals, or elsewhere. The anastomotic site is FDG-quiet, reassuring against local recurrence.

In summary, imaging confirms clear-cut disease progression: hepatic progression (though lesions remain modest in absolute size) plus new pulmonary metastases consistent with M1b disease, in the context of rising tumor markers.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

IMPRESSION: Mr. Okafor is a 58-year-old male with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, SMAD4 loss, PIK3CA E545K, PTEN loss) who has developed unequivocal disease progression on maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus bev. He now has hepatic progression (all four prior lesions slightly larger) plus new FDG-avid bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic spread, representing a change from M1a (hepatic-only) to M1b (hepatic plus pulmonary) disease. Rising CEA (62.3 ng/mL) and CA 19-9 (148 U/mL) are concordant with imaging findings. His ECOG PS remains 1 and he is overall tolerating his situation with remarkable resilience.

The decision-making for second-line systemic therapy in this patient is informed by several key factors:

1. KRAS G12D mutation: Anti-EGFR agents (Erbitux/Erbitux, Vectibix/Vectibix) remain absolutely contraindicated given RAS-mutant disease. KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors (such as investigational sotorasib-based combinations or adagrasib-based studies) are being evaluated in clinical trials for colorectal cancer, but none are currently approved as a standard second-line treatment specifically in this setting. The patient has been entered in our tumor registry and the early-phase trials team has been notified for potential trial enrollment as an option.

2. bev progression: He progressed on a bev-containing maintenance regimen. For second-line therapy following Avastin-based first-line treatment, the RAISE trial demonstrated a survival benefit for FOLFIRI plus ramu (8 mg/kg IV) compared to FOLFIRI plus placebo. The VELOUR data with aflibercept plus FOLFIRI is also an option. Continuing bev with a cytotoxic switch (FOLFIRI plus Avastin) is an alternative per some guidelines and the TML/ML18147 data, but given that progression occurred on Avastin-containing therapy, switching to Cyramza (a distinct anti-angiogenic targeting VEGFR2) is the preferred approach at this institution.

3. iri has not been used previously; he received FOLFOX-based therapy (Eloxatin + 5-FU) in the first line. Therefore, FOLFIRI brings a new cytotoxic mechanism (topoisomerase I inhibition) to the table.

4. MSS status precludes checkpoint immunotherapy (pembro, Opdivo) in the absence of tumor-specific qualifying mutations. TMB was low (8 mut/Mb) and he would not be expected to benefit from pembro based on MSS/TMB-low profile.

5. SMAD4 loss in the context of overall trajectory is noted as a factor associated with more aggressive biology and poorer prognosis, but does not alter current therapeutic selection.

6. UGT1A1 genotyping is being ordered today prior to Camptosar initiation. Given his known pre-existing hepatic lesions, LFTs are within normal limits, which is reassuring for iri dosing. Will await UGT1A1 result before finalizing iri starting dose.

PLAN:

1. SECOND-LINE THERAPY -- FOLFIRI PLUS Cyramza: Plan to initiate FOLFIRI plus ramu at the next available infusion slot (within 7-10 days, pending UGT1A1 results and port flushing). Planned regimen: iri 180 mg/m2 IV day 1 (dose to be confirmed pending UGT1A1 result -- will reduce to 150 mg/m2 if homozygous UGT1A1*28/*28), leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1 followed by 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours, and ramu 8 mg/kg IV day 1, every 14 days. BSA 2.01 m2 (unchanged).

2. ANTIEMETIC PROPHYLAXIS: Given higher emetogenic risk with iri versus prior 5-FU maintenance, prophylaxis will be upgraded to include aprepitant 125 mg PO day 1 / 80 mg day 2 and 3 (or fosaprepitant IV alternative on infusion day), plus ondansetron 8 mg IV/PO and dexamethasone 12 mg IV on day 1. Lorazepam 0.5-1 mg PO PRN breakthrough nausea. Atropine 0.25 mg subcutaneous available at infusion chair for acute cholinergic reaction (early-onset diarrhea, lacrimation, flushing) during or immediately after Camptosar infusion.

3. DIARRHEA MANAGEMENT: Patient counseled extensively on high-dose loperamide protocol (4 mg at first loose stool, then 2 mg every 2 hours until diarrhea-free for 12 hours; maximum 16 mg/day). Written instructions provided. iri can cause both early cholinergic diarrhea (within minutes to hours, managed with atropine) and delayed diarrhea (days 3-10, managed with aggressive loperamide). If diarrhea is grade 2 or higher and not controlled with loperamide within 24 hours, patient instructed to call oncology triage line immediately for IV hydration consideration.

4. BLOOD PRESSURE MONITORING: Blood pressure today is 138/86 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 5 mg. Cyramza is associated with hypertension (similar to Avastin), and blood pressure management will need close monitoring during ramu-based therapy. Antihypertensive intensification anticipated. Patient instructed to continue home blood pressure monitoring twice daily and report readings above 160/100 mmHg.

5. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY: Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy from prior Eloxatin is expected to be stable on FOLFIRI-based regimen (Camptosar does not cause peripheral neuropathy). Gabapentin 300 mg TID continued. Neurology follow-up maintained. No changes to neuropathy management at this time.

6. PORT ACCESS AND MAINTENANCE: Port-a-cath maintained in right chest wall. Accessed at infusion center for Avastin and 5-FU maintenance. Will continue to use for FOLFIRI plus ramu infusions.

7. DIABETES MANAGEMENT: Fasting glucose mildly elevated at 124 mg/dL today. Metformin 1000 mg BID continued. Patient to follow up with primary care physician for HbA1c recheck; the addition of dexamethasone in premedication regimen may transiently worsen glycemic control on infusion days -- patient counseled and advised to check blood sugars on infusion days.

8. ANEMIA: Hemoglobin mildly decreased at 12.1 g/dL. Likely anemia of chronic disease. No transfusion indicated. Will monitor; will check iron studies and reticulocyte count at next visit. No ESA therapy planned.

9. CLINICAL TRIALS: Referral to early-phase clinical trials team placed. Patient is a potential candidate for investigational KRAS G12D-specific inhibitor trials in the colorectal cancer setting. Tumor registry enrollment confirmed. Will coordinate with phase I/II team for screening evaluation.

10. RESTAGING IMAGING: After approximately 4 cycles (8 weeks) of FOLFIRI plus ramu, restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast will be obtained to assess early response.

11. FOLLOW-UP: Patient to return to clinic in 2 weeks (after cycle 1 day 1 of new regimen) for toxicity check and laboratory evaluation, and again at approximately 4 weeks.

The patient and his wife were present for this discussion. The situation was explained in clear terms, including the fact that this is a change in treatment intent (we remain in the palliative setting; the goal is disease control, symptom management, and prolongation of quality life). Mr. Okafor expressed understanding and continues to express a strong desire for aggressive treatment. He asked about clinical trials and was given information about the early-phase program. He will consider his options regarding trial enrollment and follow up with that team. His wife asked about long-term prognosis; an honest but compassionate discussion was held, emphasizing that disease control is achievable but cure is not our goal in this setting. Emotional support resources (social work, palliative care consultation, patient navigator) were offered; patient declined formal palliative care consultation at this time but agreed to see the oncology social worker.

ECOG Performance Status: 1
Cancer Status: Cancer present, progressive disease (M1b -- hepatic and pulmonary metastases)
Treatment Intent: Palliative

Electronically signed -- Hematology/Oncology Attending
